# Mark 7: The Heart of the Matter

The Pharisees come to Jesus with their question ready, their offense calculated and precise. His disciples eat with unwashed hands—a violation not of God's law but of tradition, the accumulated customs the elders have piled high over generations. What begins as a ritual concern reveals itself as something far more serious. Jesus doesn't defend His disciples or explain why hand-washing matters less than they think. Instead, He turns the conversation inside out. He quotes Isaiah directly: these people draw near with their lips while their hearts remain distant. Their worship is empty theater. Then He makes the accusation inescapable. They have made a choice—consciously, deliberately—to prefer their own rules to God's actual commands. The example He gives cuts especially deep. Moses commanded them to honor father and mother, but their tradition allows someone to dedicate what should support aging parents to God instead, technically keeping the letter of the law while destroying its spirit. This is not disagreement over minor matters. This is the substitution of human authority for divine command, and Jesus names it as nothing less than the nullification of God's word.

But Jesus doesn't stop there. He speaks directly to the crowd and announces something that shatters the entire framework the Pharisees have constructed. Nothing from outside can defile a person—not unwashed hands, not forbidden foods, not external contact with anything deemed unclean. The old system of purity laws, the careful separation of clean from unclean through externals, is being redrawn. What actually matters is what comes from within: the evil thoughts, the sexual immorality, the theft, murder, and envy that originate in human hearts. This is the real defilement. The disciples themselves struggle with this teaching, and Jesus has to speak it plainly again—a reminder that even those closest to Him can miss what He is saying. The Pharisees have invested themselves in controlling the outside, believing that keeping their hands and cups and couches clean would somehow make them pure. Jesus reveals that they have been looking in the wrong direction entirely. Purity is a matter of the heart, and only God can see and transform what is truly there.

The rest of the chapter shows Jesus living out this reality in surprising ways. He goes to Gentile territory and encounters a Syrophoenician woman whose daughter is possessed by an unclean spirit. She breaks all the rules—she approaches Him directly, she is a foreigner, she seeks help from a Jewish teacher. By the old logic, contact with her would defile Him. Jesus' initial response to her seems harsh, calling the Gentiles "dogs" and saying the children's bread is not for them. Yet she persists with a truth He has just finished teaching: even the dogs eat the crumbs that fall from the table. She understands that what matters is not ritual boundary but genuine need and faith. Jesus heals her daughter immediately, and His tone shifts. She has understood something the Pharisees missed entirely. Later, in the Decapolis, Jesus heals a deaf and mute man through physical touch and the word "Ephphatha"—be opened. The people respond with amazement: He has done all things well. These healings are not incidental to the teaching; they demonstrate it. Jesus moves freely across boundaries the Pharisees had drawn, touches those considered unclean, and restores them without concern for ritual defilement. His power flows not from external separation but from the authority of His word and the compassion of His heart.

What Mark is showing you is a fundamental reorientation of what it means to be clean, to be acceptable to God, to be part of His people. The issue is not whether you have washed your hands but whether your heart is turned toward God or away from Him. The issue is not whether you avoid contact with unclean things but whether you are willing to step across human boundaries to serve someone in need. This chapter demands that you examine where your own obedience actually rests. Do you keep the rules you can see and measure while neglecting the condition of your own heart? Do you use tradition—even good traditions—as a substitute for genuine devotion? Are there people you have marked as outside God's concern because they don't fit the categories you've constructed? Jesus calls you back again and again to what matters: a heart that listens to God's voice, that seeks His kingdom rather than its own purity, that recognizes its own need and reaches out in faith. Everything else follows from that.
